coredata.dc:title: A carbon dots-enhanced laccase-based electrochemical sensor for highly sensitive detection of dopamine in human serum
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Ru
Yu, Sha
Chen, Siyu
Dang, Yuan
Wen, Shao-Hua
Tang, Jieli
Zhou, Yuanzhen
Zhu, Jun-Jie
coredata.dc:description: Enzyme-based electrochemical sensor possesses a significant advantage in the highly efficient detection of small molecules, however, the poor electron transport efficiency limits their wide application. In this study, taking advantage of the distinct biocatalytic activity of laccase and the excellent electroconductibility of carbon dots, a carbon dots-enhanced laccase-based electrochemical sensor for the detection of dopamine (DA) is established. Thereinto, laccase can specifically recognize DA and promote its electrocatalytic oxidation on the electrode, while, the carbon dots can be used as the immobilization substrate of laccase and enhance its electron transfer efficiency, thus achieving the highly sensitive detection of dopamine. The electrochemical performance of the modified electrode interface is studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and differential pulse voltammetry. As demonstrated, the electrocatalytic activity of the proposed electrochemical sensor for DA is significantly improved and exhibits a low detection limit (0.08 μM) and a wide linear range (0.25 μM–76.81 μM). The excellent selectivity allows the sensor has the capacity for specific discrimination the DA from other interferents. Furthermore, by analyzing the DA in human serum verifies the practicability of this assay in real sample analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enzyme-based electrochemical sensor
Carbon dots
Laccase
Dopamine
High sensitivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the modified working electrode
Determination of DA in human serum
Construction of the CDs-enhanced laccase-based electrochemical sensor
Kinetic studies of the modified electrode interface reaction
Analytical performance of the electrochemical sensor
Repeatability and stability of the electrochemical sensor
Electrochemical sensing of DA in real samples

Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Shaanxi Provincial Natural Science Basic Research Program-Joint Funds of Department of Science and Technology of Shaanxi Province and Shaanxi Coal and Chemical Industry Group Co. Ltd.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province ( 2020JM-480 ) and the Key Scientific Research Project of Shaanxi Provincial Department of Education ( 20JS074 ). We also thank the financial support from State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science ( SKLACLS2204 ), the Shaanxi Provincial Natural Science Basic Research Program-Joint Funds of Department of Science and Technology of Shaanxi Province and Shaanxi Coal and Chemical Industry Group Co., Ltd . ( 2021JLM-29 ).

Dopamine hydrochloride was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Candle soot was collected from the incomplete burning of candles. Laccase was purchased from Zhengzhou Acme Chemical Co. Nitric acid (HNO3), acetic acid (CH3COOH), potassium hydroxide (KOH) and phosphoric acid (H3PO4) were purchased from Sinopharm Reagent Beijing Co., Ltd. Chitosan and Nafion solution were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Potassium chloride (KCl), potassium ferrocyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), potassium ferricyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]), dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) and potassium dihydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4) were purchased from Tianjin Hongyan Chemical Reagent Factory. 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) was used as a supporting electrolyte. The healthy human serum sample was obtained from a local laboratory and frozed at 4 °C. Ultrapure water from a Millipore Autopure system (18.2 MU) was used to prepare all the solutions.We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (2020JM-480) and the Key Scientific Research Project of Shaanxi Provincial Department of Education (20JS074). We also thank the financial support from State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2204), the Shaanxi Provincial Natural Science Basic Research Program-Joint Funds of Department of Science and Technology of Shaanxi Province and Shaanxi Coal and Chemical Industry Group Co. Ltd. (2021JLM-29).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematics of the construction of the CDs-enhanced laccase-based electrochemical sensor for DA detection. (B) The XRD spectra and (C) the FT-IR spectra of the prepared CDs. (D) EIS in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− and (E) DPV responsesion 0.1 M PBS containing 30 μM DA (pH 7.0) were measured for different electrodes: (a) bare GCE, (b) CDs/GCE, (c) Lc/GCE, (d) Lc/CDs/GCE.

(A) The CV curves of DA (50 μM) on the Lc/CDs/GCE interface in different pH with the scan rate of 100 mV s−1. From left to right: 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, and 9.0. (B) The effect of pH on peak potentials. (C) The CV curves of DA (50 μM) on the Lc/CDs/GCE interface with different scan rates in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). From bottom to top: 40, 70, 100, 130, 160, 190, 220, 250, 280, 310, 340, 370, 400 mV s−1. (D) The effect of scan rate on peak potentials.

(A) DPV curves of this biosensor in the presence of DA at different concentrations. (B) The corresponding calibration curve of peak currents at 0.12 V versus the logarithm of DA concentrations. The regression equation are Ipa = 2.972 + 3.586log c (R2 = 0.9935, DA concentration in the range of 0.25 μM–9.82 μM) and Ipa = −5.645 + 11.977log c (R2 = 0.9971, DA concentration in the range of 9.82 μM–76.81 μM). (C) Selectivity of the sensing platform toward (a) DA, (b) UA, (c) AA and (d) their mixture. (D) Anti-interference of the sensing platform toward various ions and amino acids, the concentration of DA is 20 μM. Error bars represent standard deviations from three repeated measurements.

(A) DPV response of six batch-modified electrodes in the same condition, the concentration of DA is 20 μM. (B) The DPV response of DA (20 μM) on the same modified electrode after five cycles. Error bars represent standard deviations from three repeated measurements.
(A) Schematics of the probable electrocatalytic mechanism of the proposed electrochemical sensor for DA detection. (B) The electrocatalytic process of DA on the Lc/CDs/GCE interface.
Comparison of different sensing platform for DA detection.
Spike-recovery experiments for DA detection in healthy human serum.

head.ce:title: A carbon dots-enhanced laccase-based electrochemical sensor for highly sensitive detection of dopamine in human serum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi'an University of Architecture and Technology, Xi'an, 710055, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Xi'an University of Architecture and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi'an University of Architecture and Technology, Xi'an, 710055, (P. R. China)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing, 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing, 210023, (P. R. China)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi'an University of Architecture and Technology, Xi'an, 710055, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Xi'an University of Architecture and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: R. Wu and S. Yu contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enzyme-based electrochemical sensor possesses a significant advantage in the highly efficient detection of small molecules, however, the poor electron transport efficiency limits their wide application. In this study, taking advantage of the distinct biocatalytic activity of laccase and the excellent electroconductibility of carbon dots, a carbon dots-enhanced laccase-based electrochemical sensor for the detection of dopamine (DA) is established. Thereinto, laccase can specifically recognize DA and promote its electrocatalytic oxidation on the electrode, while, the carbon dots can be used as the immobilization substrate of laccase and enhance its electron transfer efficiency, thus achieving the highly sensitive detection of dopamine. The electrochemical performance of the modified electrode interface is studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and differential pulse voltammetry. As demonstrated, the electrocatalytic activity of the proposed electrochemical sensor for DA is significantly improved and exhibits a low detection limit (0.08 μM) and a wide linear range (0.25 μM–76.81 μM). The excellent selectivity allows the sensor has the capacity for specific discrimination the DA from other interferents. Furthermore, by analyzing the DA in human serum verifies the practicability of this assay in real sample analysis.

A laccase-based electrochemical sensor by using carbon dots as electron transport promoter for highly sensitive detection of dopamine.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is an organic compound belonging to the catecholamine group, which is an important neurotransmitter [1], and an imbalance of dopamine levels in the blood can cause serious health hazards [2,3]. Therefore, accurate detection of DA content is essential for the identification of diseases in organisms. Electrochemical methods are currently the most attractive methods for the detection of DA due to their fast response time, low cost, simple operation, and high sensitivity [4]. In recent years, a variety of electrode materials, such as polymers, carbon nanotubes, metal or metallic oxide, and their composites have been developed to construct high-performance electrochemical sensors for the efficient detection of DA [5]. However, the sensitivity and selectivity of these electrochemical sensors are still the fundamental problems limiting their development [6].

The development of enzyme-based electrochemical sensors has provided significant help to improve these problems. For instance, Tang and co-workers designed a novel photoelectrochemical (PEC) enzyme immunoassay by integrating the near-infrared light-excited core-core-shell UCNP@Au@CdS upconversion nanospheres and glucose oxidase (GOx)-polyclonal anti-alpha-fetoprotein (anti-AFP) antibody system to achieve the ultrasensitive detection of AFP [7]. Subsequently, this group successively established an interdigitated capacitance immunosensing system based enzymatic biocatalytic precipitation on micro-comb electrode and an innovative bio-etching photoelectrochemical sensor model by using enzyme-encapsulated DNA hydrogels combined with In2O3–CdS nanocomposites for the sensitive detection of prostate-specific antigen in human serum samples and high-precision quantification of glucose [8,9], respectively. Therefore, the enzyme-based electrochemical sensors play an increasingly important role in the highly sensitive and specific analysis of small molecules. Recently, the laccase, that is a polyphenol oxidase (ρ-diphenol oxidase) containing four copper ions in the form of a monomeric glycoprotein and can use the unique redox ability of copper ions to catalyze a wide range of aromatic substrates [10], has been used to develop efficient sensors, especially the electrochemical sensors for highly sensitive detection of organic pollutants, dyes, and other biological components due to its unique catalytic properties [11–13]. In addition, the specific recognition ability of laccase to its catalyze substrates makes the laccase-based electrochemical sensors highly selective [14]. For instance, laccases can selectively oxidize phenolic compound that occurs at T1 copper center [11], take this advantage, Zhou and co-works constructed a novel immobilized laccase biosensor by immobilizing laccase on magnetic mesoporous microspheres (Fe3O4@SiO2@vmSiO2) and stabilizing it on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode. Thus, high sensitivity and selective electrochemical detection of DA has been achieved [15]. Unfortunately, although laccase possesses the tempting catalytic activity, it is prone to strong adsorption on a bare electrode, leading to the passivated electrode surface and greatly reducing the electron transfer efficiency and posing an obstacle to improving the electrochemical signal response [16]. Therefore, the development of a high-efficiency electron transfer interface for improving the response intensity is in urgent demand in laccase-based electrochemical sensors.

Many researchers showed that immobilization of enzymes onto nanomaterials can not only fully expose the active site, but also maintain the inherent configuration of the enzyme, which thus provides a new idea for the preparation of high-performance enzyme-based electrochemical biosensors [17]. Zhang's group prepared a highly sensitive enzyme-based electrochemical sensing platform by combining the gold nanopolymer and laccase. The introduction of gold nanopolymer greatly improved the electrochemical performance, leading to the detection of catechins with low detection limit, high selectivity, and reproducibility [10]. Among many nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) has demonstrated its excellent electrochemical properties, including high specific surface area, excellent chemical stability and electroconductibility [18,19]. For instance, Liu's group established an electrochemical sensor based on a nafion-supported nitrogen-doped CDs/Cu2O composite for the detection of chlorpromazine. The high electroconductibility and specific surface area of nitrogen-doped CDs as well as the excellent catalytic activity of Cu2O enable this sensor with wide linear range, high sensitivity, and selectivity [20]. In addition, the raw materials of CDs are widely available and easily prepared, meanwhile, the low toxicity and high biocompatibility make it ideal materials for replacing the quantum dots currently in use and developing harmless and environmentally friendly sensing platforms with high-performance. For instance, the CDs/g-C3N4 nanoheterostructures has been constructed the photoelectrochemical sensing platform for sensitive detection of the disease-related biomarkers. In this study, the combination of CDs with g-C3N4 could promote the photoexcited electron/hole separation, thus largely increasing the photocurrents [21]. Moreover, the CDs-functionalized MnO2 nanosheets (CDs-MnO2) has been also used to fabricate a simultaneously visual and PEC immunosensing system for rapid sensitive detection of Aflatoxin B1 in foodstuff [22], which is easily extended to screen other small molecules or mycotoxins by controlling the corresponding antibody, thereby possessing the excellent versatility.

Herein, we fabricate a simple and reliable CDs-enhanced laccase-based electrochemical sensor for highly sensitive detection of DA based on the excellent catalytic activity and high selectivity of laccase as well as the high electron transfer efficiency of CDs. The fabricated process of this sensor is monitored by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and its analytical performance is investigated through differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). Then, by analyzing the DA in human serum verifies the proposed sensing platform practicability in real sample analysis. We also discuss the catalytic mechanism of the laccase-CDs modified electrode to DA and why the electrocatalytic performance improved greatly in detail.

Dopamine hydrochloride was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co.. Candle soot was collected from the incomplete burning of candles. Laccase was purchased from Zhengzhou Acme Chemical Co.. Nitric acid (HNO3), acetic acid (CH3COOH), potassium hydroxide (KOH) and phosphoric acid (H3PO4) were purchased from Sinopharm Reagent Beijing Co., Ltd. Chitosan and Nafion solution were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Potassium chloride (KCl), potassium ferrocyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), potassium ferricyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]), dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) and potassium dihydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4) were purchased from Tianjin Hongyan Chemical Reagent Factory. 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) was used as a supporting electrolyte. The healthy human serum sample was obtained from a local laboratory and frozed at 4 °C. Ultrapure water from a Millipore Autopure system (18.2 MU) was used to prepare all the solutions.

Powder x-ray diffraction (XRD) maps were collected with a D8-Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker Co., Germany) from 10° to 70°. The infrared spectra were obtained on a Nicolet Magna-IR 550 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer and KBr as the reference sample within the wavelength range of 500–4000 cm−1. Raman spectra were obtained on DXR spectrometer with the 532 nm laser. All the electrochemical experiments were performed on a CHI660E electrochemical work station (Shanghai Chenhua Instruments, China).

Referring to the method of synthesizing CDs in the literature [23], the top-down method was used to synthesize CDs as follows: 0.1 g candle soot and 60 mL 5 M HNO3 were added to a three-neck flask and mixed well, and then refluxed at 100 °C for 12 h. The product was centrifuged twice at 3000 rpm for 10 min and collected the supernatant with light brown. Subsequently, the supernatant was dialyzed in dialysis bag (MD44, MW: 1000D) for 1 day, and then the solution was freeze-dried to obtain the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the modified working electrode
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was used as the working electrode. The specific modification process was as follows. The bare GCE was first polished to a mirror-like surface with 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina oxide slurries for 5 min, respectively. The polished electrode was successively washed with ethanol and ultrapure water for 5 min by sonication to remove residual alumina powder. Then, 2.0 mg CDs was suspended in 1 mL of 0.4% chitosan acetic acid solution and sonicated for 10 min until obtaining a homogeneous solution. Next, 10.0 μL CDs solution was dropped on the clean GCE and dried at room temperature to obtain the CDs modified GCE (CDs/GCE). Subsequently, 8.0 μL laccase (Lc, 3.4 mg mL−1) was dropped on the CDs/GCE, after drying at 4 °C, 5.0 μL 5% Nafion solution was further dropped to reinforce the modified GCE. After drying at 4 °C, the CDs and Lc modified GCE (Lc/CDs/GCE) was obtained.

All the electrochemical experiments were obtained on the CHI660E electrochemical work station with a three-electrode system comprised of a platinum wire (Pt) as the counter electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference electrode and the modified GCE as working electrode. EIS measurements were carried out in the frequency range from 10 kHz to 0.01 Hz at a potential of 180 mV in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−. The scan range of cyclic voltammetry (CV) was −0.1 V–0.6 V with 100 mV s−1 in PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4). The DPV was carried out in PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4), and the scan range was −0.3 V–0.3 V at a pulse amplitude of 100 mV, pulse width of 0.2 s, and a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. All the electrochemical measurements were carried out at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DA in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure of real sample analysis was according to the previous report [24]. First, the collected healthy human serum was diluted ten-fold with 0.1 M PBS to produce 10% human serum samples. The 10% healthy human serum without DA as a control (that is the original concentration of DA in human serum is 0 μM), spike-recovery experiments were performed by adding different amounts of DA (the theoretical concentrations of DA in human serum are 1 μM, 10 μM, and 20 μM, respectively) in 10% healthy human serum. Then, the current responses of these samples were monitored via the proposed electrochemical sensor. The actual concentration of DA in 10% healthy human serum could be calculated by the standard curve obtained from Fig. 3B. The recovery rates of these samples were calculated in three parallel assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the CDs-enhanced laccase-based electrochemical sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the CDs was synthesized from candle ash by nitric acid oxidation. Then, the CDs and laccase were successively modified on the electrode and finished the construction of the CDs-enhanced laccase-based electrochemical sensor interface for DA detection (Fig. 1 A). The prepared CDs was first characterized by XRD spectra, as shown in Fig. 1B, a high-intensity diffraction peak at 2θ = 22.80° and a weaker diffraction peak at 2θ = 38.40° could be attributed to the (002) and the (101) diffraction peak of graphitic carbon, respectively, which was consistent with the previous reports in the literature [23,25,26]. The vibrational properties of CDs were investigated by Raman spectroscopy, two visible peaks at 1383 cm−1 and 1603 cm−1 could be observed in Fig. S1, which corresponded to the D-band and G-band characteristic peaks, where the D-band corresponded to the disordered structure of sp2 clustered crystals and the G-band corresponded to the in-plane stretching vibrational mode E2g of single-crystal graphite [23]. All of these results demonstrate that the CDs has been successfully synthesized. In addition, the FT-IR spectroscopy was used to investigate the surface functional groups of CDs (Fig. 1C). The O–H and N–H bonds exhibited a broad absorption in the broad band in the range of 3300–3600 cm−1. The CO, O–H and N–H bonds bending vibration phenomena in the range of 1500–1700 cm−1, while the C–N bond showed a small absorption peak in the range of 1200–1360 cm−1. All of these indicate that the surface of CDs may contain carboxylic acids (-COOH), alcohol functional groups (-OH) and amino functional groups (NH2) [23,27]. The extensive presence of negative electric groups (including –COOH and –OH) on the surface of CDs can specifically interact with the amino groups on DA surface and enrich a large amount of DA, thus enhancing the possibility of redox reactions on the electrode surface [28,29].

The modified process of electrode and the effect of CDs on the electron transfer efficiency of laccase was explored by using the EIS, which can reflect the electron transfer resistance value (Ret) via the diameter of impedance spectrum [30]. As shown in Fig. 1D, in comparison with the diameter in the impedance spectrum of the bare GCE (curve a, Ret≈160 Ω), the diameter of CDs modified electrode (CDs/GCE, curve b, Ret≈50 Ω) decreased because of the excellent conductivity of CDs. When laccase was directly modified on the GCE (Lc/GCE), the diameter in the impedance spectrum increased rapidly (curve c, Ret>1000 Ω), indicating its very poor electron transfer efficiency, whereas, upon the GCE modified with both CDs and laccase (Lc/CDs/GCE), a significantly reduced diameter in the impedance spectrum (curve d, Ret≈100 Ω), which verified that CDs can be as the electron transfer mediator to enhance the electron transfer efficiency between laccase and electrode surface. All of these demonstrate that the CDs-enhanced laccase-based electrochemical sensor has been constructed successfully and the Lc/CDs/GCE interface can accelerate the redox reaction.

Moreover, the current response of DA on different modified interfaces were investigated by DPV method. As shown in Fig. 1E, compared with the DPV response of DA on the Lc/CDs/GCE interface (curve d), the DPV responses of CDs/GCE and Lc/GCE interfaces (curves b and c, respectively) looked similar to the bare GCE (curve a) that had little current response. All of these results indicate that the interface only modified with the CDs or laccase has no obvious electrocatalytic effect on DA, while the constructed Lc/CDs/GCE interface possesses a significantly enhanced electrocatalytic capacity for DA. Therefore, it is speculated that the introduction of the CDs can greatly improve the electron transfer efficiency of laccase and expand the effective electrocatalytic site of DA on the electrode surface, further indicating the feasibility of this sensing platform for DA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic studies of the modified electrode interface reaction
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of solutions pH on the electrochemical behavior of DA on the Lc/CDs/GCE interface was investigated by a CV. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the redox peak potential was gradually shifted negatively as the pH increased from 3.0 to 9.0, suggesting that the redox reaction of DA was accompanied by the transfer of protons [31,32]. In addition, we investigated the linear relationship between peak potential and pH. The oxidation peak potential is termed as Epa, while the reduction peak potential is termed as Epc. From Fig. 2B, these two peak potentials showed a good linear relationship with the variation of pH and the corresponding regression equations were Epa = 0.383–0.020 pH (R2 = 0.9943), Epc = 0.291–0.025 pH (R2 = 0.9905), respectively. Remarkably, the corresponding slope values of the Epa-pH plot and Epc-pH plot were −25.29 mV/pH and −20.14 mV/pH, respectively, which were close to half of the theoretical value of −58 mV/pH for the Nernst equation at 20 °C [31], demonstrating that the redox reaction of DA on the Lc/CDs/GCE interface was a reversible electron transfer process coupled to protons. Considering the subsequent practical application, a pH value of 7.0 was selected for the subsequent determination, which is similar to the physiological environment.

Moreover, the reaction kinetics of DA on the Lc/CDs/GCE interface was further investigated by exploring the effect of CV scan rate on the electrochemical response signal. Fig. 2C showed the CV curves of DA on the Lc/CDs/GCE interface for different scan rates. The redox peak current intensity of DA was sequentially increased with the scan rate increasing (from 40 to 400 mV s−1). As shown in Fig. 2D, the logarithmic value of scan rate (log υ) exhibited a good linear relationship with the peak potential (Ep) at υ > 160 mV s−1, and the specific regression equation were Epa = −0.078 + 0.073log υ (R2 = 0.9917) and Epc = 0.259–0.039log υ, (R2 = 0.9929), respectively. The calculated transfer coefficient (α) and the number of electron transfers (n) were calculated according to Laviron's theory as follows [33]:
(1)
E
p a
=
E
0 ′
+
2.3 R T
（ 1 − α ） n F
l o g
υ
(2)
E
p c
=
E
0 ′
−
2.3 R T
α n F
l o g
υ

Based on the conversion of the slope values, the calculation gives α and n as 0.66 and 2, respectively, indicating that the reaction is a two-electron accompanied by a two-proton transfer. Furthermore, the log υ and the logarithmic value of the peak current (log Ip) also showed a good linear relationship (Fig. S2), and the specific equation of linear regression was log Ipc = −3.799 + 1.053log υ (R2 = 0.9972), log Ipa = −5.467 + 1.492log υ (R2 = 0.9968). All of these results and the previous reports, the electrocatalytic oxidation process of DA on the Lc/CDs/GCE interface is controlled by adsorption [24].

The electrocatalytic process and mechanism of DA on the Lc/CDs/GCE interface are predicted by combining the above experimental results with literature reports. The electrocatalytic process is presumed to be dominated by two main factors. On the one hand, the negative functional groups on CDs can adsorb the DA with amine functional group via electrostatic interaction, which thus accumulates plenty of DA on the electrode interface [28,29], which thus accumulates plenty of DA on the electrode surface and facilitates its electrocatalytic reaction. Meanwhile, CDs with large specific surface area can provide more reaction active sites to promote electron exchange [34]. Thus greatly improving the electron transfer efficiency between laccase and the electrode interface (Scheme 1 A). Therefore, a strong DA response signal can be obtained when the CDs and laccase are coexist on the electrode interface.

On the other hand, laccase with unique redox ability can catalyze the DA to water [35]. In detail, DA (1) was first oxidized to dopamine-o-quinone (DOQ, 2) by reversible electrochemical or irreversible chemical means catalyzed by laccase, accompanied by the reduction of O2 (reaction Ⅰ) [36]. The resulting DOQ undergoes deprotonation and intramolecular Michael addition reactions to form 5,6-dihydroxyindole (3) (reaction Ⅱ) [37]. Next, the resulting 5,6-dihydroxyindole (3) was further converted to 5,6-dihydroxy indolyl quinone (4) by disproportionation reactions (reaction Ⅲ) and/or laccase catalyzed reactions (reaction Ⅳ) (Scheme 1B) [38,39]. In this electrocatalytic process, the laccase plays a dominant role in DA catalysis, while the CDs acts as an electron medium to accelerate the electrocatalytic process of laccase. Based on these advantages, the possibility of highly sensitive detection of DA by using the CDs-enhanced laccase-based electrochemical sensing platform can be realized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the electrochemical sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity and dynamic range of the proposed electrochemical sensor is evaluated by monitoring the DPV responses to various concentrations of DA. As shown in Fig. 3A, the oxidation peak current (Ipa) of DA increased with its concentration increasing. Noticeably, the calibration curve of peak currents at 0.12 V versus the logarithm of DA concentrations showed two linear regions of DA calibration curve (Fig. 3B), just as earlier reported literature [40,41]. It has been reported that the electrocatalytic oxidation process of DA at the electrode interface is mainly controlled by adsorption [24]. In the initial linear range (0.25–9.82 μM), the low concentration enables DA slow to enrich at the electrode interface, thus resulting in a slower reaction rate. The second linear range (9.82–76.81 μM) could be observed at higher concentrations with a increase in sensitivity, which may be attributed the accelerated enrichment processes, thus largely improving the reaction efficiency. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.08 μM (S/N = 3), which is competitive with or even superior to previously reported electrochemical and fluorescent sensing platform for DA detection (Table 1 ). The excellent electrocatalytic ability of the Lc/CDs/GCE interface for DA may be attributed to these two aspects: (1) the prepared CDs has a larger specific surface area, which can provide more DA electrocatalysis active sites on the sensing interface; (2) CDs can increase the electron transport efficiency of laccase on the modified interface, thus improving the electrocatalytic activity of laccase to DA.

The selectivity of this electrochemical platform is further investigated by recording the peak currents of DA (20 μM), uric acid (UA, 2 mM), ascorbic acid (AA, 2 mM), and the mixture of these three species. As shown in Fig. 3C, compared with the DPV responses of DA and the mixture containing DA, the others have almost no DPV responses. In addition, the anti-interference was verified in the various ions, polysaccharides and amino acids mixture containing DA (20 μM), including Na+ (145 mM), Ca2+ (2.5 mM), K+ (5 mM), Cl− (106 mM), glycine (Gly, 231 μM), glucose (Glc, 6.1 mM), and alanine (Ala, 363 μM) (Fig. 3D), which are present in large quantities in human serum [31,41]. All of these results indicate the excellent selectivity and specificity of the proposed platform toward well distinguishing the DA from other interferential substance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability and stability of the electrochemical sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility of the established sensor is evaluated by testing the DA with six batch-modified electrodes in the same condition, respectively. From Fig. 4 A, The DPV signal intensity of different batch-modified electrodes displayed no significant changes, indicating a good reproducibility of this sensor. Moreover, the stability of this electrochemical sensor is explored by monitoring the DPV response of DA (20 μM) on the same modified electrode after five cycles. As shown in Fig. 4B, the peak current of DA only decreased by ∼8% after five cycles, indicating the constructed sensor has excellent stability. Furthermore, the Lc/CDs/GCE interface still showed excellent stability for DA (3 μM) after being stored at 4 °C for 8 days (Fig. S3), demonstrating the modified electrode can be prepared in batches and stored at 4 °C for further use, thereby simplified the experimental operations and shortened the preparation time. All of these results demonstrate that the electrochemical sensor possesses excellent stability and repeatability, which is beneficial to the analysis of the real samples in complex environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical sensing of DA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the feasibility of the proposed sensor for the real sample analysis, various known concentrations of DA are diluted in 10% healthy human serum for carrying out spike-recovery experiment, the 10% healthy human serum without DA is used as the control. It has been reported that the expression level of DA range from 0.01 to 2.29 nmol/g in human pulmonary vasculature, 1.62–5.47 nmol/g in rat pulmonary vasculature and 0.02–1.1 nmol/g in rabbit pulmonary vasculature [51]. However, the expression level of DA is influenced by multiple factors, and there is no definitive value for the amount of DA in human body. In the previously reported sensors for the spike-recovery experiments of DA detection in real samples, the spiked concentration is determined according to the linear range of sensors [24,31]. Hence, according to the two linear ranges (0.25–9.82 μM and 9.82–76.81 μM) of this sensor, the low, intermediate, and high concentrations (that is 1 μM, 10 μM, 20 μM, respectively) are chosen as the spiked concentrations of the analyte. The corresponding recovery was calculated for the sample in three parallel assays. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of DA were in the range of 101.9%–103.5% with a relative standard deviation (RSD) between 0.53% to 1.01%, indicating this method has acceptable accuracy for DA detection in the complex sample.

In summary, a novel enzyme-based electrochemical sensor has been established by integration the laccase with unique catalytic activity with the CDs with glorious electroconductibility. The electrochemical sensor possesses a low detection limit, wide linear range as well as the high sensitivity and selectivity. In addition, the reliable reproducibility and stability enable this sensor to quantitative detection DA, even in the complex human serum environment. The excellent analytical performance of the proposed electrochemical sensor is primarily dependent on the highly electrocatalytic activity of the modified electrode interface for DA, which may be attributed to the CDs can effectively enhance the electron transfer efficiency of laccase on the electrode interface. Therefore, our proposed electrochemical sensing platform will provide an important tool for the accurate detection of bioactive small molecules (such as hormones and small drug molecules) in the field of bioanalytical science. Admittedly, the laccase is directly modified on the electrode surface in this study, which confined laccase to the two-dimensional electrode interface and shielded its electrocatalytic activity. Therefore, extension the encapsulation carrier of laccase to three-dimensional space will be of great significance for improving its electrocatalytic activity.

Ru Wu: and. Sha Yu: conceived and designed the experiments and performed all experimental work, wrote the original draft. Siyu Chen: analyzed the data. Yuan Dang: and. Shao-Hua Wen: revised the draft preliminarily. Jieli Tang: and. Yuanzhen Zhou: and. Jun-Jie Zhu: revised the draft elaborately. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (2020JM-480) and the Key Scientific Research Project of Shaanxi Provincial Department of Education (20JS074). We also thank the financial support from State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2204), the Shaanxi Provincial Natural Science Basic Research Program-Joint Funds of Department of Science and Technology of Shaanxi Province and Shaanxi Coal and Chemical Industry Group Co., Ltd. (2021JLM-29).

ce:section.ce:para:
